---
title: "Unbalance & Critical Speed Problem"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
---

A shaft of negligible mass is simply supported at both ends and carries a
single disc of mass **8 kg** at its mid-span. The disc has a small residual
unbalance such that its centre of mass is **0.5 mm** from the geometric axis
of the shaft. The static deflection of the shaft at mid-span under the disc's
weight is **1.2 mm**. The shaft runs at **1 400 rpm**.

1. Calculate the **critical speed** of the shaft.
2. Calculate the **out-of-balance force** at the running speed.
3. Determine whether the shaft is running **sub- or supercritically**.

## Given Data

| Symbol | Description | Value |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| $m$ | Disc mass | 8 kg |
| $e$ | Eccentricity (CG offset) | 0.5 mm = 0.0005 m |
| $\delta_{st}$ | Static deflection at mid-span | 1.2 mm = 0.0012 m |
| $N$ | Running speed | 1 400 rpm |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | 9.81 m/s² |

## Critical Speed

Using the static-deflection formula:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.0012}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{8175} = \frac{60}{2\pi} \times 90.42$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx 864 \ \text{rpm}}$$

## Out-of-Balance Force at Running Speed

### Angular velocity at running speed

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1400}{60} = 146.61 \ \text{rad/s}$$

### Out-of-balance force

The centrifugal force due to the eccentric mass is:

$$F = me\omega^2 = 8 \times 0.0005 \times 146.61^2$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 86.1 \ \text{N}}$$

## Sub- or Supercritical?

$$N = 1400 \ \text{rpm} \quad > \quad N_c = 864 \ \text{rpm}$$

The shaft is running **supercritically** — above its first critical speed.
In this regime the disc self-centres: the centre of mass migrates toward the
bearing axis, and the dynamic behaviour stabilises. The shaft will have passed
through the critical speed during run-up, where transient amplitudes must be
managed by sufficient run-up rate or damping.

The speed ratio is:

$$\frac{N}{N_c} = \frac{1400}{864} \approx 1.62$$

Running at 1.62 times the critical speed places the shaft comfortably in the
supercritical region. A ratio of at least 1.2–1.3 is generally recommended
to ensure stable supercritical operation.

## Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Critical speed $N_c$ | **864 rpm** |
| Running speed $N$ | 1 400 rpm |
| Speed ratio $N/N_c$ | 1.62 — supercritical |
| Angular velocity $\omega$ | 146.61 rad/s |
| Out-of-balance force $F$ | **86.1 N** |

## Verification (Python)

```python
import numpy as np

# Given
m          = 8.0       # kg
e          = 0.0005    # m  (0.5 mm eccentricity)
delta_st   = 0.0012    # m  (1.2 mm static deflection)
N_run      = 1400      # rpm
g          = 9.81      # m/s^2

# Part 1 — critical speed
N_c = (60 / (2 * np.pi)) * np.sqrt(g / delta_st)
print(f"Critical speed  N_c = {N_c:.1f} rpm")

# Part 2 — out-of-balance force at running speed
omega = 2 * np.pi * N_run / 60
F     = m * e * omega**2
print(f"Angular velocity ω  = {omega:.2f} rad/s")
print(f"Out-of-balance force F = {F:.1f} N")

# Part 3 — speed ratio
ratio = N_run / N_c
regime = "supercritical" if N_run > N_c else "subcritical"
print(f"\nSpeed ratio N/N_c   = {ratio:.2f}  →  {regime}")
```
